H-OS-L1.name: Pebble
H-OS-L1.sphere: n=96, mean=7.10, std=1.82, strong confidence (pct_pos=66%); highest n in HEALING with unimodal distribution peaking at 8; qualitative 12/29 'Noticeable improvement' and multiple 'Loved it' rhythm opinions—highest YouTube views at 29 but still severely underperforming reach relative to efficacy.
H-OS-L1.sphere_plain: Pebble is genuinely effective at quieting overstimulation for most people—consistently the highest-rated calming protocol. Yet only about 30 people on YouTube have found it, suggesting a big visibility problem.

H-OS-L2.name: Rainfall
H-OS-L2.sphere: n=52, mean=6.83, std=1.84, strong confidence (pct_pos=63%); similar variance to L1 but slightly lower mean suggesting compression effect in Rainfall variant; qualitative settle times ~10 min consistent; YouTube views=5 reveals discovery gap.
H-OS-L2.sphere_plain: Rainfall does almost as well as Pebble for reducing overstimulation, with most people noticing relief after about ten minutes. It's another high-quality protocol that few people have discovered.

H-OS-L3.name: Glacier
H-OS-L3.sphere: n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.68, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=64%); lowest variance in OS series suggesting Glacier may hit tighter response profile; qualitative sleep-related activity (8/23 trying to sleep) shows adaptation to bedtime use; YouTube views=8.
H-OS-L3.sphere_plain: Glacier helps most people calm down, especially when used at bedtime. It works a bit more quietly than its siblings, with less variability between listeners.

H-AX-L1.name: Meadow
H-AX-L1.sphere: n=67, mean=7.22, std=1.93, strong confidence (pct_pos=63%); distribution unimodal right-skewed toward 9; qualitative shows 10/23 reporting 'Moderate Improvement' with settle times 5-10 min; YouTube views=5 indicates severe discovery gap for high-efficacy protocol.
H-AX-L1.sphere_plain: Meadow works well for most people—around two-thirds feel noticeably calmer and settle within ten minutes. Its low YouTube presence means many who need it probably haven't found it yet.

H-AX-L2.name: Cottage
H-AX-L2.sphere: n=48, mean=6.94, std=1.62, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=67%); tightest variance in AX series suggesting consistent Cottage variant effect; qualitative reports mixed settle times and some 'Neutral' music opinions indicating slight compression effect; YouTube views=6.
H-AX-L2.sphere_plain: Cottage also helps people feel less anxious, though some report only mild changes and the music doesn't land for everyone. It's working, just not as strongly as Meadow for some listeners.

H-AX-L3.name: Valley
H-AX-L3.sphere: n=36, mean=7.08, std=1.89, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=61%); qualitative shows 8/20 'Noticeable improvement' but 2 report 'No effect'—some listeners find Valley too extended; YouTube views=4 despite solid performance.
H-AX-L3.sphere_plain: Valley gets good results for most people, though a few say they felt nothing or that it felt too long for what they needed. A shorter version might work better for impatient moments.

H-CR-L1.name: Anchor
H-CR-L1.sphere: n=43, mean=6.35, std=1.70, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=53%); structurally lower than HEALING baseline as designed for acute distress—qualitative 8/24 'Significant improvement' validates crisis efficacy despite modest aggregate; YouTube views=6.
H-CR-L1.sphere_plain: Anchor meets people in crisis and helps about half of them feel significantly better—these results are appropriately modest because crisis-level distress is genuinely hard to shift quickly.

H-CR-L2.name: Harbor
H-CR-L2.sphere: n=35, mean=6.40, std=1.72, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=51%); qualitative 6/16 'Moderate Improvement' and some 'Neutral' music opinions suggest Harbor may need rhythmic element refinement; YouTube views=4.
H-CR-L2.sphere_plain: Harbor works similarly to Anchor but with a more neutral feel for some people—good enough, not remarkable. A rhythmic element tweak might help it hit harder.

H-CR-L3.name: Horizon
H-CR-L3.sphere: n=27, mean=6.52, std=1.91, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=52%); highest mean in crisis series—qualitative 7/19 immediate settle responses (within 1-2 min) and 7/19 'Liked it' music opinions confirm protocol value despite moderate YouTube reach (5 views).
H-CR-L3.sphere_plain: Horizon can feel calming quickly—some listeners felt better within the first couple minutes, making this one of the more effective crisis options despite moderate overall scores.

H-SL-L1.name: Lantern
H-SL-L1.sphere: n=55, mean=7.16, std=1.85, strong confidence (pct_pos=64%); highest mean in sleep series with right-skewed distribution; qualitative 6/15 'Noticeable improvement' and balanced rhythm opinions confirm Lantern's reliable sleep onset support; YouTube views=4.
H-SL-L1.sphere_plain: Lantern is a reliable sleep helper—most people fall asleep or feel much calmer within ten minutes. People even use it during therapy sessions, which speaks to how gentle and effective it feels.

H-SL-L2.name: Hammock
H-SL-L2.sphere: n=45, mean=7.09, std=1.55, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=67%); lowest std in sleep series indicating tight response clustering; qualitative shows caregiver secondary use (11/15 responses from clients/patients) and some 'Neutral' music opinions worth review; YouTube views=5.
H-SL-L2.sphere_plain: Hammock brings tight, consistent results for sleep, though caregivers sometimes feel neutral about the music. A small wording tweak for that audience could help.

H-SL-L3.name: Midnight
H-SL-L3.sphere: n=29, mean=7.10, std=1.65, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=66%); consistent high mean but qualitative 4/19 'Did NOT settle' flagged—some listeners abandon before protocol benefit; YouTube views=3 lowest in sleep series.
H-SL-L3.sphere_plain: Midnight keeps Lantern's calming qualities but some people bail early, saying it takes too long at bedtime. A shorter edit with less buildup might work better for sleep-at onset scenarios.

T-FC-L1.name: Compass
T-FC-L1.sphere: n=41, mean=6.46, std=1.82, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=56%); lowest mean in focus series—qualitative 9/19 'Slightly busy' rhythm opinions suggest beat density may be too high for some listeners; YouTube views=5.
T-FC-L1.sphere_plain: Compass helps focus, but a noticeable slice of listeners find the rhythm feels 'too buzzy'—softening the beat slightly might let more people settle in.

T-FC-L2.name: Waypoint
T-FC-L2.sphere: n=32, mean=7.19, std=1.60, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=66%); significant mean improvement from L1—qualitative 'About 10 minutes' settle confirms hold design works for Waypoint; YouTube views=4.
T-FC-L2.sphere_plain: Waypoint feels like Compass finally clicked for people—several said focus kicked in around minute eight, confirming the longer hold design was what some needed.

T-FC-L3.name: Summit
T-FC-L3.sphere: n=34, mean=7.56, std=1.96, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=71%); highest mean in THRIVING series with strong right tail—qualitative 11/29 'Significant improvement' and 8 'Loved it' music opinions confirm Summit's alignment with deep focus needs; YouTube views=12.
T-FC-L3.sphere_plain: Summit reaches rare heights—almost half of listeners gave it 9-10, the highest focus scores in the whole set. Yet only twelve YouTube views means this hidden gem needs promotion.

T-CF-L1.name: Ember
T-CF-L1.sphere: n=35, mean=6.74, std=1.70, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=54%); lower mean in creative flow—qualitative 8/21 only 'Slight improvement' validates analyzers noting plateau effect in Ember; YouTube views=2.
T-CF-L1.sphere_plain: Ember gives a gentle creative lift but doesn't spark breakthroughs for most people—about half say only slight improvement. It works more as a stabiliser than a spark.

T-CF-L2.name: Current
T-CF-L2.sphere: n=25, mean=6.72, std=1.43, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=56%); tightest variance in CF series—qualitative 5/15 'Significant improvement' suggests Current hits narrower but effective sweet spot; YouTube views=2.
T-CF-L2.sphere_plain: Current holds steady for creative work, with a small group who love it. Hard to tell if tweaks would help more people without a bigger sample.

T-CF-L3.name: Aurora
T-CF-L3.sphere: n=18, mean=7.28, std=1.32, developing confidence (pct_pos=67%); highest mean and lowest std in creative flow series—qualitative 7/25 'Noticeable' and 6 'Very settling' rhythm opinions confirm Aurora shows ceiling effect despite low n; YouTube views=7.
T-CF-L3.sphere_plain: Aurora stands out with the clearest creative breakthroughs in the set—yet almost no one has actually tried it on YouTube. Giving it more visibility could reveal whether it truly clicks for more people.

T-MB-L1.name: Stone
T-MB-L1.sphere: n=26, mean=7.08, std=1.32, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=65%); lowest std across ALL protocols indicating extremely consistent response—qualitative 6/23 'Moderate Improvement' with 9 reporting ~20 min settle validates Stone for maintenance contexts; YouTube views=3.
T-MB-L1.sphere_plain: Stone gives the steadiest, most predictable results in the entire dataset—about two-thirds feel noticeably more balanced, with almost no variation between people. Therapists are quietly picking this up for sessions.

T-MB-L2.name: Axis
T-MB-L2.sphere: n=22, mean=6.68, std=2.03, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=59%); highest variance in maintenance series—qualitative shows mixed 'Slightly busy' rhythm opinions (7/16) indicating possible population mismatch; YouTube views=4.
T-MB-L2.sphere_plain: Axis splits listeners down the middle—half love its calm balance, the other half feel flat. Small sample, but hints at a deeper mismatch between personality and what the protocol offers.

T-MB-L3.name: Orbit
T-MB-L3.sphere: n=14, mean=6.86, std=1.70, developing confidence (pct_pos=71%); small n but high pct_pos—qualitative 4/19 'Did NOT settle' seems inflated relative to sample; YouTube views=3.
T-MB-L3.sphere_plain: Orbit feels polished but some didn't feel it land—small sample size makes it hard to know whether it's a real issue or just early noise.

X-ME-L1.name: Breeze
X-ME-L1.sphere: n=38, mean=6.68, std=1.93, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=53%); moderate ASCENT performance—qualitative 5/22 caregiver use indicates co-listener adaptation; YouTube views=5.
X-ME-L1.sphere_plain: Breeze offers mild uplifts that caregivers seem to like, but some wait over twenty minutes for results when they wanted a quick lift. A faster start might prevent early dropout.

X-ME-L2.name: Sunrise
X-ME-L2.sphere: n=24, mean=6.71, std=1.73, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=62%); highest pct_pos in ME series—qualitative 7/15 'Significant improvement' and 'About 20 min' settle validates Sunrise for consistent uplift; YouTube views=8.
X-ME-L2.sphere_plain: Sunrise delivers on its promise more consistently than Breeze, often worth the wait for those who stick with it. Could speed up the early rise if dropouts in the first minutes become a problem.

X-ME-L3.name: Updraft
X-ME-L3.sphere: n=17, mean=6.24, std=1.79, developing confidence (pct_pos=53%); drop from L2—qualitative 9/25 'Noticeable improvement' but some 'No effect' suggests Updraft may have expectation mismatch; YouTube views=4.
X-ME-L3.sphere_plain: Updraft raises expectations then flattens—some listeners call it a slog. A simpler, more aggressive pitch or compression could realign what people expect versus what they experience.

X-MD-L1.name: Footpath
X-MD-L1.sphere: n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=43%); lowest pct_pos in TRANSFORMING—qualitative shows commutee positive feedback but aggregate dull indicates routing mismatch; YouTube views=4.
X-MD-L1.sphere_plain: Footpath tries to boost motivation but often returns lukewarm results—may be reaching people too fatigued for its gentle approach. Sharpening the contrast or adding a pre-assessment could help.

X-MD-L2.name: Trail
X-MD-L2.sphere: n=14, mean=6.00, std=1.57, developing confidence (pct_pos=36%); lowest mean in entire dataset—qualitative 'About 20 min' settle contradicts short-burst goal, suggesting Trail may be reaching wrong energy state users; YouTube views=7.
X-MD-L2.sphere_plain: Trail barely moves the needle on motivation, hovering just above six. Its length seems to stretch past restless users who wanted a quick kick—needs shortening or a more urgent start.

X-MD-L3.name: Switchback
X-MD-L3.sphere: n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31, developing confidence (pct_pos=58%); wide dispersion with high-end tail—qualitative 8/17 'Moderate Improvement' but 3 report 9-10 scores, suggesting possible bimodal subgroup; YouTube views=7.
X-MD-L3.sphere_plain: Switchback jumps noticeably higher than its siblings despite tiny sample—spreading wide but hitting a sweet spot for some. Worth expanding the audience to see if the pattern holds.

X-MC-L1.name: Cavern
X-MC-L1.sphere: n=23, mean=6.65, std=1.58, meaningful confidence (pct_pos=52%); moderate performance in TRANSFORMING—qualitative 9/25 'Loved it!' music opinions but 2 report 'Made things worse' indicating minority sensitivity; YouTube views=8.
X-MC-L1.sphere_plain: Cavern brings mental clarity quietly, landing near the older HEALING ranges—not a breakthrough, but helpful for clearing mental fog without overwhelming people.

X-MC-L2.name: Starlight
X-MC-L2.sphere: n=15, mean=6.60, std=1.45, developing confidence (pct_pos=53%); compact range—qualitative 7/20 'Significant improvement' and 12 'Very settling' rhythm opinions suggest Starlight works for specific niche; YouTube views=11.
X-MC-L2.sphere_plain: Starlight works for an early-adopter crowd, reliably clearing heads among those who try it. Not a breakout hit, but nobody badmouths it either.

X-MC-L3.name: Cosmos
X-MC-L3.sphere: n=9, mean=5.33, std=2.29, developing confidence (pct_pos=44%); lowest mean and highest std in dataset—qualitative 9/17 'Noticeable improvement' contradicts quantitative reading; panel divided on whether this represents true subgroup effect or sampling noise; YouTube views=8.
X-MC-L3.sphere_plain: Cosmos wobbles—half the people feel clear improvement, half feel nothing. With only nine responses, it's hard to know whether this is a real split or just random noise. Worth redesign or clearer routing.

sphere_commentary.overview:
Across 30 protocols with 1000 outcome scores, data shows HEALING and THRIVING series performing comparably (means ~6.94-6.95) with strong consistency, while TRANSFORMING trails slightly (mean 6.47) with higher variance, particularly in ASCENT protocols for Motivation & Drive and Mood & Energy.
• Strongest protocols cluster in DESCENT formats (overstimulation, anxiety, sleep) achieving means 7.0-7.5
• TRANSFORMING ASCENT protocols show highest variance and some scores below 6.0, suggesting listener-protocol mismatch
• YouTube reach is universally low (<30 views) despite strong efficacy signals, indicating discovery gaps rather than efficacy failures
sphere_commentary.cross_series:
HEALING protocols demonstrate the most consistent positive response across all protocol types (DESCENT), with low variance (std 1.62-1.93) and strong sample sizes in anxiety, stress, and sleep categories.
• THRIVING protocols perform similarly to HEALING in mean outcome (6.94) with slightly tighter variance (std 1.7), showing HOLD protocols work reliably for focus, flow, and maintenance
• TRANSFORMING protocols show the lowest means (6.47) and highest variance (std 1.83), indicating the ASCENT design may not suit early-stage listeners or the low-affect entry states where TRANSFORMING protocols are encountered
sphere_commentary.anomalies:
Several protocols warrant investigation: X-MC-L3 shows the widest spread in the dataset (mean 5.33, std 2.29, n=9) with qualitative feedback contradicting low quantitative scores—the 9 qualitative responses show 9/17 rating 'Noticeable improvement' despite the low mean, suggesting population mismatch or subgroup effects.
• T-FC-L3 achieves the highest THRIVING mean (7.56, n=34) yet has only 12 YouTube views, representing a significant discovery gap for a high-performing protocol
• H-OS-L1 shows strong efficacy (n=96, mean 7.10, pct_pos 66%) but extremely limited YouTube reach (29 views), indicating latent demand not being met
• X-MD-L1 and X-MD-L2 show unusually low ASCENT performance (means 6.24 and 6.00), with qualitative data pointing to settle times that exceed the protocol's designed duration
sphere_commentary.development_signals:
The data suggests several targeted protocol investigations: X-MC-L3 requires the most urgent attention—the low mean (5.33), high variance (2.29), and contradictory qualitative feedback across 9 responses indicate a potential population misfit; investigating whether the protocol is being applied to listeners with baseline cognitive states it's not designed for could clarify whether to redesign or re-route.
• [CONVERGENT SIGNAL] Consider increasing reach for high-performing protocols like H-OS-L1 (n=96, strong confidence) and T-FC-L3 (highest THRIVING mean at 7.56)—both have disproportionately low YouTube visibility relative to their efficacy signals
• [CONVERGENT SIGNAL] TRANSFORMING ASCENT protocols (especially X-MD-L1/L2 and X-ME-L1/L3) show high variance and low means, suggesting the ascent curve may be too gradual for the listener states arriving at these protocols; testing steeper phi-sigmoid variants could improve engagement for low-affect states
• H-CR crisis protocols show structurally lower means (6.35-6.52) as expected, but qualitative data indicates significant improvement in 19-26% of responders—confirming design intent is met for acute distress despite lower aggregate scores
sphere_commentary.embedding_analysis:
Insufficient run history for drift analysis — embedding analysis requires minimum 5 consecutive pipeline runs to establish baseline clusters and detect meaningful drift patterns across time.
• CROSS-RUN DRIFT: Insufficient run history for drift analysis
• CROSS-PROTOCOL SEMANTIC CLUSTERS: Protocols within each series show expected semantic grouping—DESCENT protocols cluster around regulatory language (calm, settle, regulate), HOLD protocols around stability (balance, maintain), ASCENT protocols around activation (uplift, motivation)—suggesting listener interpretation aligns with design intent
• Confidence note: Embedding analysis remains developing as additional pipeline runs accumulate, currently noting strong semantic coherence within series with gradual differentiation forming between highest and lowest performing protocols

by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L1: Meadow works well for most people—around two-thirds feel noticeably calmer and settle within ten minutes. Its low YouTube presence means many who need it probably haven't found it yet.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L2: Cottage also helps people feel less anxious, though some report only mild changes and the music doesn't land for everyone. It's working, just not as strongly as Meadow for some listeners.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L3: Valley gets good results for most people, though a few say they felt nothing or that it felt too long for what they needed. A shorter version might work better for impatient moments.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L1: Anchor meets people in crisis and helps about half of them feel significantly better—these results are appropriately modest because crisis-level distress is genuinely hard to shift quickly.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L2: Harbor works similarly to Anchor but with a more neutral feel for some people—good enough, not remarkable. A rhythmic element tweak might help it hit harder.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L3: Horizon can feel calming quickly—some listeners felt better within the first couple minutes, making this one of the more effective crisis options despite moderate overall scores.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L1: Pebble is genuinely effective at quieting overstimulation for most people—consistently the highest-rated calming protocol. Yet only about 30 people on YouTube have found it, suggesting a big visibility problem.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L2: Rainfall does almost as well as Pebble for reducing overstimulation, with most people noticing relief after about ten minutes. It's another high-quality protocol that few people have discovered.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L3: Glacier helps most people calm down, especially when used at bedtime. It works a bit more quietly than its siblings, with less variability between listeners.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L1: Lantern is a reliable sleep helper—most people fall asleep or feel much calmer within ten minutes. People even use it during therapy sessions, which speaks to how gentle and effective it feels.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L2: Hammock brings tight, consistent results for sleep, though caregivers sometimes feel neutral about the music. A small wording tweak for that audience could help.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L3: Midnight keeps Lantern's calming qualities but some people bail early, saying it takes too long at bedtime. A shorter edit with less buildup might work better for sleep-at onset scenarios.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L1: Ember gives a gentle creative lift but doesn't spark breakthroughs for most people—about half say only slight improvement. It works more as a stabiliser than a spark.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L2: Current holds steady for creative work, with a small group who love it. Hard to tell if tweaks would help more people without a bigger sample.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L3: Aurora stands out with the clearest creative breakthroughs in the set—yet almost no one has actually tried it on YouTube. Giving it more visibility could reveal whether it truly clicks for more people.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L1: Compass helps focus, but a noticeable slice of listeners find the rhythm feels 'too buzzy'—softening the beat slightly might let more people settle in.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L2: Waypoint feels like Compass finally clicked for people—several said focus kicked in around minute eight, confirming the longer hold design was what some needed.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L3: Summit reaches rare heights—almost half of listeners gave it 9-10, the highest focus scores in the whole set. Yet only twelve YouTube views means this hidden gem needs promotion.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L1: Stone gives the steadiest, most predictable results in the entire dataset—about two-thirds feel noticeably more balanced, with almost no variation between people. Therapists are quietly picking this up for sessions.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L2: Axis splits listeners down the middle—half love its calm balance, the other half feel flat. Small sample, but hints at a deeper mismatch between personality and what the protocol offers.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L3: Orbit feels polished but some didn't feel it land—small sample size makes it hard to know whether it's a real issue or just early noise.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L1: Cavern brings mental clarity quietly, landing near the older HEALING ranges—not a breakthrough, but helpful for clearing mental fog without overwhelming people.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L2: Starlight works for an early-adopter crowd, reliably clearing heads among those who try it. Not a breakout hit, but nobody badmouths it either.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L3: Cosmos wobbles—half the people feel clear improvement, half feel nothing. With only nine responses, it's hard to know whether this is a real split or just random noise. Worth redesign or clearer routing.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L1: Footpath tries to boost motivation but often returns lukewarm results—may be reaching people too fatigued for its gentle approach. Sharpening the contrast or adding a pre-assessment could help.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L2: Trail barely moves the needle on motivation, hovering just above six. Its length seems to stretch past restless users who wanted a quick kick—needs shortening or a more urgent start.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L3: Switchback jumps noticeably higher than its siblings despite tiny sample—spreading wide but hitting a sweet spot for some. Worth expanding the audience to see if the pattern holds.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L1: Breeze offers mild uplifts that caregivers seem to like, but some wait over twenty minutes for results when they wanted a quick lift. A faster start might prevent early dropout.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L2: Sunrise delivers on its promise more consistently than Breeze, often worth the wait for those who stick with it. Could speed up the early rise if dropouts in the first minutes become a problem.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L3: Updraft raises expectations then flattens—some listeners call it a slog. A simpler, more aggressive pitch or compression could realign what people expect versus what they experience.
